description: Bundled hydraulic cards for pump control, pressure, hand pump use, leakage logic, brakes, flaps and steering.

cards.front: What controls normal hydraulic pump operation?
cards.back: The pressure switch controls electric pump operation.

cards.front: Does the pressure switch affect the standby hand pump?
cards.back: No. The pressure switch has no effect on hand pump operation.

cards.front: Why turn off the electric pump if hydraulic leakage is suspected?
cards.back: To preserve as much hydraulic fluid as possible for hand pump operation.

cards.front: What is the approximate hydraulic reservoir capacity?
cards.back: Approximately two thirds of a U.S. gallon, just over 2 litres.

cards.front: What hydraulic fluid type is used?
cards.back: MIL-H-5606 hydraulic fluid, red in colour.

cards.front: What is the normal hydraulic pressure range?
What is an appropriate action if the hydraulic system cycles frequently?
cards.back: Turn off the electric pump and preserve fluid for hand pump operation.

cards.front: Which bus powers the hydraulic pump and hydraulic pump fail light?
cards.back: The left hand DC bus powers both the hydraulic pump and the hydraulic pump fail light.

cards.front: Should a popped hydraulic pump circuit breaker be reset in flight?
cards.back: No. The pump may have seized.

cards.front: Why may no pressure build while hand-pumping flaps if selector and indication disagree?
cards.back: Pressure cannot build until the selector valve and actual flap setting agree; pressure builds when the flaps reach the selected position, provided fluid remains.

cards.front: Name four systems using aircraft hydraulic pressure.
cards.back: Brakes, flaps, steering, and skis if installed.
